title: Multiclassing
summary: Rules for combining multiple classes to create a unique character.
content:
## Multiclassing

Multiclassing allows you to gain levels in multiple classes, creating a unique combination of abilities. When you gain a level, instead of advancing in your current class, you can take your first level in a new class — provided you meet the prerequisites.

### Prerequisites

To qualify for a new class, you must meet the ability score prerequisites for **both** your current class and your new class.

| Class | Ability Score Minimum |
|---|---|
| Barbarian | Strength 13 |
| Bard | Charisma 13 |
| Cleric | Wisdom 13 |
| Druid | Wisdom 13 |
| Fighter | Strength 13 or Dexterity 13 |
| Monk | Dexterity 13 and Wisdom 13 |
| Paladin | Strength 13 and Charisma 13 |
| Ranger | Dexterity 13 and Wisdom 13 |
| Rogue | Dexterity 13 |
| Sorcerer | Charisma 13 |
| Warlock | Charisma 13 |
| Wizard | Intelligence 13 |

### Proficiencies Gained

When you take your first level in a new class via multiclassing, you gain only **some** of that class's starting proficiencies.

| Class | Proficiencies Gained |
|---|---|
| Barbarian | Shields, simple weapons, martial weapons |
| Bard | Light armor, one skill of your choice, one musical instrument |
| Cleric | Light armor, medium armor, shields |
| Druid | Light armor, medium armor, shields (no metal) |
| Fighter | Light armor, medium armor, shields, simple weapons, martial weapons |
| Monk | Simple weapons, shortswords |
| Paladin | Light armor, medium armor, shields, simple weapons, martial weapons |
| Ranger | Light armor, medium armor, shields, simple weapons, martial weapons, one skill from the Ranger list |
| Rogue | Light armor, one skill from the Rogue list, thieves' tools |
| Sorcerer | — |
| Warlock | Light armor, simple weapons |
| Wizard | — |

### Multiclass Spell Slots

If you have levels in multiple spellcasting classes, you use the **Multiclass Spellcaster** table to determine your total spell slots. Add together all levels of bard, cleric, druid, sorcerer, and wizard. Add half your levels (rounded down) of paladin and ranger. Add one-third your levels (rounded down) of Eldritch Knight fighter or Arcane Trickster rogue.

| Combined Level | 1st | 2nd | 3rd | 4th | 5th | 6th | 7th | 8th | 9th |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | 3 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 3 | 4 | 2 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 4 | 4 | 3 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 6 | 4 | 3 | 3 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 7 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 1 | — | — | — | — | — |
| 8 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 2 | — | — | — | — | — |
| 9 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 1 | — | — | — | — |
| 10 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | — | — | — | — |
| 11 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | — | — | — |
| 12 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | — | — | — |
| 13 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | — | — |
| 14 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | — | — |
| 15 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | — |
| 16 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | — |
| 17 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 18 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 19 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| 20 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 1 | 1 |

### Pact Magic and Spellcasting

Warlock spell slots from **Pact Magic** are tracked **separately** from other spellcasting slots. Pact Magic slots recover on a short rest and are always cast at the warlock's Pact Magic slot level. However, you can use Pact Magic slots to cast spells from other classes and vice versa.

### How Class Features Interact

- **Extra Attack** does not stack. If you gain Extra Attack from more than one class, they don't add together.
- **Unarmored Defense** can only be used from one class. If you have it from both Barbarian and Monk, you choose one.
- **Channel Divinity** uses are shared if gained from multiple sources (Cleric/Paladin), but the options available come from each class separately.
- **Hit Points**: When you gain a level in any class after 1st, you gain the hit die for that class (not your first class).
- **Proficiency Bonus**: Always based on total character level, not individual class levels.

### Popular Multiclass Combinations

- **Paladin/Warlock ("Padlock")**: Short-rest Pact Magic slots fuel Divine Smite. Charisma synergy for spellcasting and Aura of Protection. Eldritch Blast gives a ranged option.
- **Fighter/Wizard ("Gish")**: Action Surge lets you cast two spells in one turn. Heavy armor proficiency protects a squishy wizard. Con save proficiency from Fighter helps concentration.
- **Rogue/Ranger**: Hunter's Mark adds damage to Sneak Attack rounds. Expertise plus ranger skills for excellent scouting. Two-weapon fighting or archery style complements Rogue.
- **Sorcerer/Warlock ("Sorlock")**: Short-rest warlock slots convert to sorcery points. Eldritch Blast + Quickened Spell for massive damage output.
- **Barbarian/Rogue**: Reckless Attack grants advantage, which enables Sneak Attack reliably. Danger Sense + Evasion (later levels) for excellent saves.
- **Cleric 1/Wizard X**: Heavy armor + shield proficiency for a wizard. Healing Word as a backup. Only delays wizard progression by one level.

title: Leveling Up
summary: What happens each time your character gains a level, including HP increases, class features, ASIs, and proficiency bonus progression.
content:
## Leveling Up

When your character earns enough experience points (or reaches a milestone), you gain a level. Each level brings new capabilities and increased survivability.

### What Happens Each Level

1. **Choose a class** to gain a level in (usually your current class, or a new class if multiclassing).
2. **Gain hit points**: Roll your class's Hit Die and add your Constitution modifier (minimum 1 HP gained). Alternatively, take the **average** (rounded up) + Con modifier.
3. **Gain class features** listed for that level in your class table.
4. **Check for Ability Score Improvements** (ASI) — most classes get them at levels 4, 8, 12, 16, and 19.
5. **Check for proficiency bonus increase** — it goes up at levels 5, 9, 13, and 17.

### Hit Die Averages by Class

| Hit Die | Average (rounded up) |
|---|---|
| d6 (Sorcerer, Wizard) | 4 |
| d8 (Bard, Cleric, Druid, Monk, Rogue, Warlock) | 5 |
| d10 (Fighter, Paladin, Ranger) | 6 |
| d12 (Barbarian) | 7 |

### Proficiency Bonus by Level

| Character Level | Proficiency Bonus |
|---|---|
| 1–4 | +2 |
| 5–8 | +3 |
| 9–12 | +4 |
| 13–16 | +5 |
| 17–20 | +6 |

### Ability Score Improvements (ASI)

Most classes gain ASIs at **levels 4, 8, 12, 16, and 19**. Exceptions:
- **Fighter**: Additional ASIs at levels 6 and 14 (total of 7).
- **Rogue**: Additional ASI at level 10 (total of 6).

Each ASI lets you increase one ability score by 2 or two ability scores by 1 each (maximum 20). Alternatively, you can take a **feat** instead of the ASI (if your DM allows feats).

### Feat vs ASI: When to Choose What

- **Take the ASI** if your primary ability score is below 16, or if you want to round out an odd score for a +1 modifier increase.
- **Take a feat** if your primary ability is already 18+ or if a feat dramatically improves your build (e.g., Sharpshooter for archers, War Caster for concentration casters, Sentinel for front-line defenders, Polearm Master for reach builds, Great Weapon Master for heavy hitters).
- **Half-feats** (feats that also increase an ability score by 1) are excellent for rounding odd scores while gaining a powerful ability (e.g., Resilient, Fey Touched, Shadow Touched, Crusher/Slasher/Piercer).

### Milestone Leveling

Instead of tracking XP, many DMs use **milestone leveling**, where the party levels up when they accomplish significant story goals. Benefits:
- No need to track XP after every encounter.
- The DM controls pacing — the party levels when narratively appropriate.
- Players don't feel pressured to fight every encounter for XP.
- Works especially well for roleplay-heavy or narrative campaigns.

### Key Level Breakpoints

- **Level 1**: Starting capabilities — fragile, limited resources.
- **Level 3**: Most subclass choices. Characters start to feel distinct.
- **Level 5**: Major power spike — Extra Attack, 3rd-level spells, proficiency bonus increases to +3.
- **Level 9**: Proficiency bonus increases to +4. 5th-level spells available.
- **Level 11**: Another major power spike — 6th-level spells, third Extra Attack for fighters.
- **Level 17**: Proficiency bonus reaches +6. 9th-level spells available. Near peak power.
- **Level 20**: Capstone class features. Maximum power.
tags: leveling up,advancement,hit points,ASI,ability score improvement,feat,proficiency bonus,milestone

title: Experience Points Table
summary: XP thresholds for each level from 1 to 20, plus XP awarded by monster challenge rating and encounter division rules.
content:
## Experience Points Table

Experience Points (XP) measure your character's progress toward the next level. When you accumulate enough XP, you level up.

### XP Thresholds by Level

| Level | XP Required | XP to Next Level |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 300 |
| 2 | 300 | 600 |
| 3 | 900 | 1,800 |
| 4 | 2,700 | 3,800 |
| 5 | 6,500 | 7,500 |
| 6 | 14,000 | 9,000 |
| 7 | 23,000 | 11,000 |
| 8 | 34,000 | 14,000 |
| 9 | 48,000 | 16,000 |
| 10 | 64,000 | 21,000 |
| 11 | 85,000 | 15,000 |
| 12 | 100,000 | 20,000 |
| 13 | 120,000 | 20,000 |
| 14 | 140,000 | 25,000 |
| 15 | 165,000 | 30,000 |
| 16 | 195,000 | 30,000 |
| 17 | 225,000 | 40,000 |
| 18 | 265,000 | 40,000 |
| 19 | 305,000 | 50,000 |
| 20 | 355,000 | — |

### XP Awarded by Challenge Rating

Monsters have a Challenge Rating (CR) that determines how much XP they're worth when defeated.

| CR | XP |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 or 10 |
| 1/8 | 25 |
| 1/4 | 50 |
| 1/2 | 100 |
| 1 | 200 |
| 2 | 450 |
| 3 | 700 |
| 4 | 1,100 |
| 5 | 1,800 |
| 6 | 2,300 |
| 7 | 2,900 |
| 8 | 3,900 |
| 9 | 5,000 |
| 10 | 5,900 |
| 11 | 7,200 |
| 12 | 8,400 |
| 13 | 10,000 |
| 14 | 11,500 |
| 15 | 13,000 |
| 16 | 15,000 |
| 17 | 18,000 |
| 18 | 20,000 |
| 19 | 22,000 |
| 20 | 25,000 |
| 21 | 33,000 |
| 22 | 41,000 |
| 23 | 50,000 |
| 24 | 62,000 |
| 25 | 75,000 |
| 26 | 90,000 |
| 27 | 105,000 |
| 28 | 120,000 |
| 29 | 135,000 |
| 30 | 155,000 |

### Encounter XP Division

1. **Add up** the XP values of all monsters defeated in the encounter.
2. **Divide** the total evenly among all party members who participated.
3. Each character adds their share to their XP total.

**Note**: The encounter multiplier (used for encounter difficulty calculation) does **not** affect the actual XP awarded. It is only used by the DM to gauge difficulty when designing encounters.

### Non-Combat XP

Many DMs also award XP for:
- Completing quests or story objectives
- Creative problem solving
- Excellent roleplaying
- Overcoming traps or hazards
- Social encounters resolved through diplomacy

There are no fixed rules for non-combat XP — it's at the DM's discretion. A common guideline is to award XP equivalent to a monster of appropriate CR for the challenge overcome.

title: Character Creation Steps
summary: The complete step-by-step process for creating a D&D 5th Edition character from scratch.
content:
## Character Creation Steps

Creating a D&D character involves several steps. Here's the full process from start to finish.

### Step 1: Choose a Race

Your race determines your character's physical traits, some innate abilities, and cultural background.

- **Racial Ability Score Increases**: Most races increase one or more ability scores (e.g., Dwarf gets +2 Constitution).
- **Size**: Most player races are Medium. Halflings and Gnomes are Small.
- **Speed**: Typically 25 or 30 feet of walking speed.
- **Racial Traits**: Darkvision, resistances, proficiencies, and other unique abilities.
- **Languages**: Common plus one or more additional languages.
- **Subrace**: Many races have subraces with additional traits (e.g., Hill Dwarf vs. Mountain Dwarf).

### Step 2: Choose a Class

Your class is the primary definition of what your character can do — the source of your abilities, features, and role in the party.

- Determine your **hit die** (affects HP).
- Note your **saving throw proficiencies** (two per class).
- Choose your **skills** from the class list.
- Record **starting equipment** or roll for gold.
- Note any **1st-level class features** (e.g., Rage for Barbarian, Spellcasting for Wizard).

### Step 3: Determine Ability Scores

Your six ability scores define your character's basic capabilities:

- **Strength (STR)**: Physical power, melee attacks, carrying capacity.
- **Dexterity (DEX)**: Agility, reflexes, ranged attacks, AC, initiative.
- **Constitution (CON)**: Health, stamina, hit points, concentration saves.
- **Intelligence (INT)**: Memory, reasoning, Wizard spellcasting.
- **Wisdom (WIS)**: Perception, intuition, Cleric/Druid/Ranger spellcasting.
- **Charisma (CHA)**: Force of personality, Bard/Paladin/Sorcerer/Warlock spellcasting.

Generate scores using one of three methods: **Standard Array**, **Point Buy**, or **Rolling** (see Ability Score Generation for details). Then assign them to the abilities that best support your class and playstyle. Apply racial ability score increases.

### Step 4: Describe Your Character

Flesh out your character's identity:

- **Name**: Choose something fitting for your race and setting.
- **Alignment**: Your moral and ethical outlook (Lawful/Neutral/Chaotic + Good/Neutral/Evil).
- **Background**: Provides two skill proficiencies, tool or language proficiencies, starting equipment, and a feature. Also gives personality traits, ideals, bonds, and flaws.
- **Appearance**: Age, height, weight, eye color, hair, distinguishing features.
- **Backstory**: Where you came from and why you're adventuring.

### Step 5: Choose Equipment

You can either take the **starting equipment** provided by your class and background, or roll for **starting gold** and buy equipment from the equipment lists.

| Class | Starting Gold (if rolling) |
|---|---|
| Barbarian | 2d4 × 10 gp |
| Bard | 5d4 × 10 gp |
| Cleric | 5d4 × 10 gp |
| Druid | 2d4 × 10 gp |
| Fighter | 5d4 × 10 gp |
| Monk | 5d4 gp |
| Paladin | 5d4 × 10 gp |
| Ranger | 5d4 × 10 gp |
| Rogue | 4d4 × 10 gp |
| Sorcerer | 3d4 × 10 gp |
| Warlock | 4d4 × 10 gp |
| Wizard | 4d4 × 10 gp |

### Final Calculations

After all choices are made, calculate:

- **Ability Modifiers**: (Score − 10) ÷ 2, rounded down.
- **Hit Points**: Hit Die maximum + Constitution modifier at 1st level.
- **Armor Class (AC)**: Depends on armor worn and Dexterity modifier.
- **Initiative**: Equal to your Dexterity modifier.
- **Attack Bonuses**: Ability modifier + proficiency bonus for weapons you're proficient with.
- **Spell Save DC**: 8 + proficiency bonus + spellcasting ability modifier.
- **Spell Attack Modifier**: Proficiency bonus + spellcasting ability modifier.
- **Passive Perception**: 10 + Wisdom modifier (+ proficiency if proficient in Perception).

title: Ability Score Generation
summary: Three methods for generating ability scores: Standard Array, Point Buy, and Rolling (4d6 drop lowest), with pros and cons of each.
content:
## Ability Score Generation

There are three common methods for generating the six ability scores that define your character. Your DM will tell you which method to use.

### Method 1: Standard Array

Assign the following six numbers to your abilities in any order:

**15, 14, 13, 12, 10, 8**

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Fast and simple | No chance of very high scores |
| Every character is equally capable | Every character has the same total |
| Balanced — no one is overpowered or underpowered | Can feel less exciting than rolling |
| Great for new players | Less customizable than point buy |

### Method 2: Point Buy

You have **27 points** to spend. All abilities start at 8. Increasing an ability score costs points as follows:

| Score | Point Cost | Total Spent (from 8) |
|---|---|---|
| 8 | 0 | 0 |
| 9 | 1 | 1 |
| 10 | 2 | 2 |
| 11 | 3 | 3 |
| 12 | 4 | 4 |
| 13 | 5 | 5 |
| 14 | 7 | 7 |
| 15 | 9 | 9 |

Scores cannot go below 8 or above 15 before racial bonuses.

**Common Point Buy Arrays**:
- **15/15/15/8/8/8** — Maximize three key scores, dump the rest.
- **15/14/13/12/10/8** — Identical to Standard Array (a coincidence).
- **15/15/13/10/10/8** — Strong in two abilities, decent elsewhere.
- **15/14/14/10/10/8** — Balanced spread with two solid secondary scores.

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Highly customizable | Slower than Standard Array |
| Still balanced — everyone has 27 points | Scores capped at 15 (before racial) |
| No luck involved | Can feel like "solving a math problem" |
| Players can optimize for their build | Less exciting than rolling |

### Method 3: Rolling (4d6 Drop Lowest)

Roll **four six-sided dice (4d6)** for each ability score. Drop the **lowest** die and sum the remaining three. Repeat six times, then assign the six results to your abilities.

**Example**: You roll 4, 3, 6, 2. Drop the 2. Your score is 4 + 3 + 6 = 13.

**Statistical Average**: The average result of 4d6 drop lowest is approximately **12.24**, giving an average total of about 73.5 (compared to 72 for Standard Array/Point Buy).

| Pros | Cons |
|---|---|
| Exciting — the anticipation of rolling | Can create power imbalance between players |
| Chance of exceptional scores (16, 17, 18) | Risk of very low scores |
| Each character feels unique | Some players may feel cheated by bad rolls |
| Traditional D&D experience | Not balanced — one player may roll much better |

### Which Method Should You Use?

- **New to D&D?** Use **Standard Array**. It's fast and gets you playing.
- **Want control over your build?** Use **Point Buy**. It's the most strategic option.
- **Want excitement and don't mind imbalance?** Use **Rolling**. It's the most fun at the table.
- **Running a one-shot?** Rolling is great — imbalance matters less in short games.
- **Running a long campaign?** Point Buy or Standard Array ensures no one feels left behind for months of play.

title: Hit Points
summary: How hit points work at 1st level and beyond, including the role of Constitution, hit dice by class, and toughness comparisons.
content:
## Hit Points

Hit points (HP) represent your character's ability to withstand damage. When your HP reaches 0, you fall unconscious and begin making death saving throws.

### HP at 1st Level

At 1st level, your hit points equal the **maximum value of your Hit Die + your Constitution modifier**.

| Class | Hit Die | HP at 1st Level |
|---|---|---|
| Barbarian | d12 | 12 + Con mod |
| Fighter, Paladin, Ranger | d10 | 10 + Con mod |
| Bard, Cleric, Druid, Monk, Rogue, Warlock | d8 | 8 + Con mod |
| Sorcerer, Wizard | d6 | 6 + Con mod |

### HP at Higher Levels

Each time you gain a level after 1st, you add HP in one of two ways:

1. **Roll your Hit Die** and add your Constitution modifier (minimum of 1 HP gained).
2. **Take the average** (rounded up) and add your Constitution modifier.

| Hit Die | Roll Range | Average |
|---|---|---|
| d6 | 1–6 | 4 |
| d8 | 1–8 | 5 |
| d10 | 1–10 | 6 |
| d12 | 1–12 | 7 |

**Tip**: Taking the average is usually recommended for consistent, reliable HP growth. Rolling is riskier — a roll of 1 feels terrible, but a max roll feels great.

### Why Constitution Matters

Constitution modifier is added to **every level's HP gain**. Over 20 levels, even a small difference adds up significantly.

**Example — HP at Level 10 (d8 Hit Die, taking averages)**:

| Con Score | Con Mod | HP at Level 10 |
|---|---|---|
| 8 | −1 | 8 + (5−1) × 9 = 44 |
| 10 | +0 | 8 + 5 × 9 = 53 |
| 12 | +1 | 8 + (5+1) × 9 = 62 |
| 14 | +2 | 8 + (5+2) × 9 = 71 |
| 16 | +3 | 8 + (5+3) × 9 = 80 |

That's a **36 HP difference** between −1 Con and +3 Con at level 10. Constitution is never a dump stat for characters who expect to be in danger.

### Toughness Comparisons by Class

Average HP at level 10 with 14 Constitution (+2 modifier), taking averages:

| Class | Hit Die | HP at Level 10 |
|---|---|---|
| Barbarian | d12 | 12 + (7+2) × 9 = 93 |
| Fighter, Paladin, Ranger | d10 | 10 + (6+2) × 9 = 82 |
| Bard, Cleric, Druid, Monk, Rogue, Warlock | d8 | 8 + (5+2) × 9 = 71 |
| Sorcerer, Wizard | d6 | 6 + (4+2) × 9 = 60 |

### Increasing Max HP Beyond Level Gains

- **Tough feat**: Grants +2 HP per level (retroactive). Equivalent to +4 Constitution for HP purposes only.
- **Increasing Constitution**: If you raise your Con modifier (via ASI or magic item), your HP increases retroactively by 1 per level for each +1 to the modifier.
- **Hill Dwarf**: Gains +1 HP per level from the Dwarven Toughness racial trait.
- **Aid spell**: Increases current and maximum HP by 5 (or more when upcast) for 8 hours.
- **Heroes' Feast spell**: Increases maximum HP by 2d10 for 24 hours.

### Hit Dice as a Resource

Your Hit Dice aren't just for leveling — they're also a healing resource. During a **short rest**, you can spend Hit Dice to recover HP. Roll the die and add your Constitution modifier — you regain that many HP. You recover **half your total Hit Dice** (minimum 1) after a long rest.

title: Archery
summary: You gain a +2 bonus to attack rolls you make with ranged weapons.
content:
## Archery

*Fighting Style*

You gain a **+2 bonus to attack rolls** you make with ranged weapons.

### Details

- Applies to all **ranged weapon attacks**, including longbow, shortbow, hand crossbow, heavy crossbow, light crossbow, darts, javelins (when thrown), and other ranged weapons.
- This is a bonus to the **attack roll** (to hit), not to damage.
- Stacks with other bonuses like proficiency, ability modifiers, and magic weapon bonuses.
- One of the strongest fighting styles because +2 to hit is a significant accuracy boost — roughly equivalent to a 10% increased chance to hit.

### Available To

- **Fighter**: Chosen at 1st level.
- **Ranger**: Chosen at 2nd level.

### Why It's Good

A +2 to hit with ranged attacks is one of the best fighting styles in the game. Ranged attackers already benefit from staying out of melee danger, and the accuracy bonus makes them even more reliable. Combined with the **Sharpshooter** feat (which lets you take −5 to hit for +10 damage), the Archery style offsets the accuracy penalty, making the feat much more effective.

### Comparison

Archery is generally considered the **strongest** fighting style because:
- Hitting more often is always valuable (a miss deals zero damage).
- The bonus is always active — no situational requirements.
- It synergizes exceptionally well with Sharpshooter and Crossbow Expert feats.

title: Defense
summary: While you are wearing armor, you gain a +1 bonus to AC.
content:
## Defense

*Fighting Style*

While you are wearing armor, you gain a **+1 bonus to AC**.

### Details

- Requires you to be **wearing armor** (light, medium, or heavy). Does not apply if you're unarmored.
- Stacks with shields, magic armor bonuses, and other AC calculations.
- A simple but consistently valuable bonus that makes you harder to hit in every combat.

### Available To

- **Fighter**: Chosen at 1st level.
- **Paladin**: Chosen at 2nd level.
- **Ranger**: Chosen at 2nd level.

### Why It's Good

A +1 to AC is roughly a **5% reduction** in incoming attacks that hit you. Over the course of an adventuring day with many combats, this adds up to significant damage prevented. It's a "set and forget" style — always active, always useful, no decisions required.

### When to Choose Defense

- When you're a **melee character** who expects to take hits regularly.
- When your build doesn't strongly benefit from another fighting style (e.g., you use a sword and shield but don't have adjacent allies to protect).
- When you want a **safe, reliable** choice that's never bad.
- Pairs well with heavy armor and shield for maximum AC (plate armor + shield + Defense = AC 21).

### Comparison

Defense is the **most universally useful** fighting style. It's never the most exciting choice, but it's never wrong either. Other styles give bigger offensive boosts, but Defense keeps you alive.

title: Dueling
summary: When you are wielding a melee weapon in one hand and no other weapons, you gain a +2 bonus to damage rolls with that weapon.
content:
## Dueling

*Fighting Style*

When you are wielding a melee weapon in one hand and no other weapons, you gain a **+2 bonus to damage rolls** with that weapon.

### Details

- You must be holding a **one-handed melee weapon** and **no other weapon** in your other hand.
- You **can** use a shield in your off hand — Dueling still applies.
- The +2 is added to **damage rolls**, not attack rolls.
- Works with versatile weapons **only when wielded in one hand** (not when used two-handed).
- Applies to every hit, making it very consistent extra damage.

### Available To

- **Fighter**: Chosen at 1st level.
- **Paladin**: Chosen at 2nd level.
- **Ranger**: Chosen at 2nd level.

### Why It's Good

Dueling provides a flat +2 damage per hit, which is highly competitive with two-handed weapon styles. A longsword (1d8) with Dueling averages **6.5 + 2 = 8.5 damage** per hit, compared to a greatsword (2d6) with Great Weapon Fighting averaging about **8.33 damage** per hit. Dueling achieves nearly the same damage while leaving a hand free for a **shield (+2 AC)**, giving you both offense and defense.

### Ideal Builds

- **Sword and board**: Longsword/warhammer + shield. Great AC with solid damage.
- **Paladin**: High damage per hit + Divine Smite makes each hit count even more.
- **Spellcaster melee**: Keeps a hand free for somatic components (or hold a focus/shield with War Caster).

title: Great Weapon Fighting
summary: When you roll a 1 or 2 on a damage die for an attack you make with a melee weapon that you are wielding with two hands, you can reroll the die and must use the new roll.
content:
## Great Weapon Fighting

*Fighting Style*

When you roll a **1 or 2** on a damage die for an attack you make with a melee weapon that you are wielding with **two hands**, you can reroll the die and must use the new roll, even if the new roll is a 1 or 2.

### Details

- The weapon must have the **two-handed** or **versatile** property (and you must be wielding it with two hands).
- You reroll each individual damage die that shows a 1 or 2. You **must** use the new result.
- You can only reroll each die **once** — if the reroll is also a 1 or 2, you're stuck with it.
- Applies only to the **weapon's damage dice**, not to extra damage from sources like Divine Smite, Sneak Attack, or other features (per common DM interpretation and Sage Advice).

### Available To

- **Fighter**: Chosen at 1st level.
- **Paladin**: Chosen at 2nd level.

### Average Damage Increase

The actual damage boost from GWF is **smaller than it appears**:

| Weapon | Normal Average | GWF Average | Increase |
|---|---|---|---|
| Greatsword (2d6) | 7.00 | 8.33 | +1.33 |
| Greataxe (1d12) | 6.50 | 7.33 | +0.83 |
| Maul (2d6) | 7.00 | 8.33 | +1.33 |
| Halberd/Glaive (1d10) | 5.50 | 6.30 | +0.80 |

GWF works better with **2d6 weapons** (greatsword, maul) because there are more dice to potentially reroll.

### Comparison to Dueling

Dueling gives a flat +2 damage per hit with a shield bonus to AC. Great Weapon Fighting gives about +1.33 damage per hit with no defensive benefit. From a pure optimization standpoint, Dueling with a shield is often more efficient. GWF is chosen for the **two-handed weapon fantasy** and synergy with feats like Great Weapon Master and Polearm Master.

metadata_json.bonus: Reroll 1s and 2s on damage dice with two-handed weapons

title: Protection
summary: When a creature you can see attacks a target other than you that is within 5 feet of you, you can use your reaction to impose disadvantage on the attack roll. You must be wielding a shield.
content:
## Protection

*Fighting Style*

When a creature you can see attacks a target other than you that is within **5 feet** of you, you can use your **reaction** to impose **disadvantage** on the attack roll. You must be wielding a **shield**.

### Details

- Costs your **reaction** — you can only use it once per round (until the start of your next turn).
- You must be wielding a **shield** to use this feature.
- The ally being protected must be **within 5 feet** of you.
- You must be able to **see** the attacking creature.
- You impose **disadvantage** on the attack roll, which is a powerful defensive effect (roughly −3.5 to the roll on average, or about a 17.5% reduced hit chance).
- Must be declared **before** the attack roll is made.

### Available To

- **Fighter**: Chosen at 1st level.
- **Paladin**: Chosen at 2nd level.

### When It's Useful

- When you're fighting alongside a **squishy ally** (wizard, sorcerer) who got caught in melee.
- When you want to play a **bodyguard or tank** character archetype.
- When the enemy makes a **single powerful attack** (disadvantage on one big swing is more impactful than on one of many small attacks).

### Limitations

- Only works against **one attack per round** (costs your reaction).
- Requires **positioning** — you and your ally must be within 5 feet.
- Doesn't help against area effects, saving throw abilities, or attacks targeting you.
- Competes with other reaction options (Opportunity Attacks, Shield spell, Counterspell, etc.).

### Comparison

Protection is generally considered one of the **weaker** fighting styles because:
- It's situational — requires specific positioning and costs your reaction.
- Defense gives a permanent +1 AC that protects **you** without any resource cost.
- Interception (from Tasha's Cauldron of Everything) is often considered a direct upgrade, reducing damage by 1d10 + proficiency instead of imposing disadvantage.

metadata_json.bonus: Reaction: impose disadvantage on attack vs. adjacent ally (requires shield)

title: Two-Weapon Fighting
summary: When you engage in two-weapon fighting, you can add your ability modifier to the damage of the second attack.
content:
## Two-Weapon Fighting

*Fighting Style*

When you engage in two-weapon fighting, you can add your **ability modifier** to the damage of the **second attack** (the bonus action attack).

### How Two-Weapon Fighting Works (Base Rules)

Without this fighting style, the base rules for two-weapon fighting are:

1. You must be holding a **light** melee weapon in each hand.
2. When you take the **Attack action** with one weapon, you can use a **bonus action** to make one attack with the other weapon.
3. You **don't add your ability modifier** to the damage of the bonus action attack (unless that modifier is negative).

### What This Fighting Style Adds

Normally, the off-hand bonus action attack does **not** include your ability modifier on damage. This fighting style removes that restriction, letting you add your STR or DEX modifier to the off-hand damage as normal.

**Example**: A fighter with 16 Dexterity (+3) wielding two shortswords (1d6 each):
- Without this style: Main hand deals 1d6+3, off-hand deals 1d6+0.
- With this style: Main hand deals 1d6+3, off-hand deals 1d6+3.

That's an extra +3 damage per round — a meaningful boost at every level.

### Available To

- **Fighter**: Chosen at 1st level.
- **Ranger**: Chosen at 2nd level.

### Weapon Options

Both weapons must have the **light** property (unless you have the Dual Wielder feat):

| Weapon | Damage | Properties |
|---|---|---|
| Shortsword | 1d6 piercing | Finesse, light |
| Scimitar | 1d6 slashing | Finesse, light |
| Handaxe | 1d6 slashing | Light, thrown |
| Dagger | 1d4 piercing | Finesse, light, thrown |
| Club | 1d4 bludgeoning | Light |

With the **Dual Wielder** feat, you can use non-light one-handed weapons (e.g., two longswords for 1d8 + mod each).

### Limitations

- Uses your **bonus action** every round for the extra attack — competes with other bonus action abilities (Hunter's Mark, Rage, Cunning Action, many spells).
- **No shield** — you're holding two weapons, so your AC is lower than sword-and-board.
- At higher levels, **Extra Attack** only applies to the Attack action, not the bonus action. You still only get one bonus action attack regardless of level.
- Falls behind other fighting styles in damage per round at higher levels because extra attacks from features like Extra Attack don't multiply the bonus action attack.

### Best Used By

- **Rangers** with Hunter's Mark (each hit triggers the extra 1d6, and an extra attack means an extra chance to deal it).
- **Rogues** (multiclassed with fighter or ranger for the style) — the extra attack gives a second chance to land Sneak Attack if the first misses.
- Low-level characters who want maximum attacks per round before Extra Attack comes online at level 5.

metadata_json.bonus: Add ability modifier to off-hand attack damage

tags: fighting style,two-weapon fighting,dual wield,bonus action,off-hand,light weapon,fighter,ranger

title: Spell Slots Explained
summary: What spell slots are, how they work, how they recover, and the difference between cantrips and leveled spells.
content:
## Spell Slots Explained

Spell slots are the fuel that powers your leveled spells. Think of them as a limited daily resource — each time you cast a leveled spell, you use up a spell slot of the appropriate level or higher.

### What Is a Spell Slot?

A spell slot is an expendable resource that represents the magical energy a caster can channel. Each slot has a **level** (1st through 9th). To cast a spell, you must expend a spell slot of the spell's level **or higher**.

- A 1st-level spell like *Magic Missile* requires a 1st-level slot (or higher).
- A 3rd-level spell like *Fireball* requires a 3rd-level slot (or higher).
- You cannot cast a spell if you don't have an available slot of sufficient level.

### How Spell Slots Recover

- **Long Rest Casters** (Bard, Cleric, Druid, Paladin, Ranger, Sorcerer, Wizard): All expended spell slots are recovered after a **long rest** (typically 8 hours).
- **Short Rest Caster** (Warlock): Pact Magic slots are recovered after a **short rest** (1 hour) or long rest. Warlocks have fewer slots (1-4) but they're all at the same high level and recharge frequently.
- **Arcane Recovery** (Wizard): Once per day after a short rest, recover spell slots with a combined level equal to or less than half your wizard level (rounded up). No 6th-level or higher slots.
- **Natural Recovery** (Druid, Circle of the Land): Similar to Arcane Recovery — recover slots during a short rest.
- **Font of Magic** (Sorcerer): Convert sorcery points into spell slots or vice versa.

### Upcasting

Many spells become more powerful when cast using a higher-level slot than required. This is called **upcasting**.

**Examples**:
- *Cure Wounds* (1st level): Heals 1d8 + mod. At 2nd level: 2d8 + mod. At 3rd level: 3d8 + mod.
- *Magic Missile* (1st level): Three darts. One additional dart per slot level above 1st.
- *Fireball* (3rd level): 8d6 damage. +1d6 per slot level above 3rd.

Not all spells benefit from upcasting. Check the spell description for "At Higher Levels" to see if it scales.

### Cantrips vs. Leveled Spells

| Feature | Cantrips | Leveled Spells |
|---|---|---|
| Slot Cost | None (free) | Requires a spell slot |
| Uses Per Day | Unlimited | Limited by available slots |
| Scaling | Automatically at levels 5, 11, 17 | Only if upcast with higher slot |
| Power Level | Lower, consistent damage/utility | Higher, more impactful effects |
| Learning | Known permanently once learned | Must be prepared or known |

Cantrips are your **at-will** magic — reliable fallbacks you can use all day without resource cost. Leveled spells are your **heavy artillery** — powerful but limited.

### Spell Slot Management Tips

- **Conserve high-level slots** for critical moments (boss fights, emergencies).
- **Use cantrips** for routine damage instead of burning slots on weak enemies.
- **Upcast efficiently** — a 2nd-level *Healing Word* on a downed ally is often better than a 1st-level *Cure Wounds* (bonus action vs. action).
- **Short rest classes** (Warlock, Wizard with Arcane Recovery) should push for short rests when possible to recover resources.
- **Know your encounter budget** — if you expect 6-8 encounters per long rest (the design assumption), ration your slots accordingly.

title: Preparing Spells
summary: The difference between prepared casters and known casters, how many spells you can prepare, how to change your prepared list, and ritual casting.
content:
## Preparing Spells

Some casters must **prepare** a list of available spells each day. Others simply **know** their spells permanently. Understanding which type you are is crucial for playing a spellcaster effectively.

### Prepared Casters vs. Known Casters

| Feature | Prepared Casters | Known Casters |
|---|---|---|
| Classes | Cleric, Druid, Paladin, Wizard | Bard, Ranger, Sorcerer, Warlock |
| Spell Access | Entire class spell list (or spellbook for Wizard) | Only spells you've chosen/learned |
| Daily Flexibility | Change prepared spells after each long rest | Swap one spell per level-up only |
| Number Available | Limited prepared list per day | All known spells always available |
| Advantage | Adaptability — prepare for what you expect | Simplicity — no daily decisions |

### How Many Spells Can You Prepare?

- **Cleric**: Wisdom modifier + Cleric level (minimum 1). Plus always-prepared domain spells.
- **Druid**: Wisdom modifier + Druid level (minimum 1). Plus always-prepared circle spells (Circle of the Land).
- **Paladin**: Charisma modifier + half Paladin level, rounded down (minimum 1). Plus always-prepared oath spells.
- **Wizard**: Intelligence modifier + Wizard level (minimum 1). But can only prepare from spells in your **spellbook**.

### Changing Your Prepared Spells

Prepared casters can change their entire prepared spell list after each **long rest**. The process takes **1 minute per spell level** for each spell on your new list.

**Practical tips**:
- Before a long rest, think about what you expect to face tomorrow.
- Keep versatile staples always prepared (e.g., *Healing Word*, *Shield of Faith* for Clerics).
- Swap in situational spells when you know you'll need them (e.g., *Water Breathing* before an underwater adventure).
- Wizards have the biggest advantage here — their spellbook can hold far more spells than they prepare, giving them the widest selection.

### Known Casters: Choosing Wisely

Known casters have a **fixed** set of spells that can only change when they level up (swap one old spell for one new spell).

| Class | Spells Known at Level 1 | Max Spells Known at Level 20 |
|---|---|---|
| Bard | 4 | 22 |
| Ranger | 2 (at level 2) | 11 |
| Sorcerer | 2 | 15 |
| Warlock | 2 | 15 (+ Mystic Arcanum) |

**Tips for known casters**:
- Choose **versatile** spells that are useful in many situations.
- Avoid overly niche spells — you can't swap them out until next level.
- Prioritize spells that **scale well** when upcast (e.g., *Hold Person* → targets more creatures at higher levels).
- Your limited spell list is a restriction, but it also means less decision-making each day.

### Always-Prepared Spells

Some spells are **always prepared** and don't count against your prepared limit:

- **Cleric Domain spells**: Each domain grants specific spells at certain cleric levels.
- **Paladin Oath spells**: Each oath grants specific spells at certain paladin levels.
- **Druid Circle spells** (Circle of the Land): Each terrain grants bonus spells.
- **Racial spells**: Some races grant spells that are always available.

These are essentially "free" prepared spells — a significant advantage.

### Ritual Casting Connection

Some prepared casters can cast certain spells as **rituals** (taking 10 extra minutes) without expending a spell slot — but only if the spell has the ritual tag. See the Ritual Casting entry for full details.

title: Spell Components
summary: The three types of spell components — Verbal (V), Somatic (S), and Material (M) — and how arcane focuses, component pouches, and costly/consumed materials work.
content:
## Spell Components

Most spells require one or more components to cast. These represent the physical requirements of channeling magical energy.

### The Three Component Types

#### Verbal (V)

The caster must speak words of power — mystic incantations, divine prayers, or arcane syllables.

- Requires the ability to **speak** (or at least vocalize).
- Cannot cast V spells while **silenced** (by the *Silence* spell or similar effects).
- Does not need to be loud, but must be audible.
- Stealth implication: Casting a V spell reveals your presence.

#### Somatic (S)

The caster must make specific gestures or movements with at least one free hand.

- Requires a **free hand** — cannot perform somatic components while both hands are occupied.
- If holding a weapon and shield, you have no free hand for somatic components.
- **War Caster feat** allows you to perform somatic components even with weapons/shield in both hands.
- A hand holding a **spellcasting focus** can also perform somatic components for spells that have **both** M and S components.

#### Material (M)

The caster must have access to specific physical materials listed in the spell description.

- Materials come in three categories (see below).
- You must have a **free hand** to access materials (the same hand used for S components works).

### Material Component Categories

#### 1. Generic Materials (No Cost Listed)

Most material components have no gold cost (e.g., "a bit of fur," "a pinch of sand"). These can be replaced by:

- **Arcane Focus**: A staff, wand, crystal, orb, or rod (for Sorcerer, Warlock, Wizard).
- **Holy Symbol**: An amulet, emblem, or reliquary (for Cleric, Paladin). Can be worn or emblazoned on a shield.
- **Druidic Focus**: A sprig of mistletoe, totem, staff, or yew wand (for Druid).
- **Component Pouch**: A small belt pouch containing all common spell components. Available to any caster.

You do **not** need to buy each individual component — the focus or pouch replaces them all.

#### 2. Costly Materials (Gold Value Listed)

Some spells require materials with a specific gold value (e.g., *Chromatic Orb* requires "a diamond worth at least 50 gp"). These components:

- **Cannot** be replaced by a focus or component pouch.
- Must be **purchased or found** during adventuring.
- Are **not consumed** unless stated — you keep the component after casting.

**Examples**:
- *Chromatic Orb*: Diamond worth 50 gp (not consumed — buy once, use forever).
- *Revivify*: Diamonds worth 300 gp (consumed — need new diamonds each casting).
- *Identify*: Pearl worth 100 gp (not consumed).

#### 3. Consumed Materials

Some spells **consume** the material component when cast (the description says "which the spell consumes").

- You need a **new component** each time you cast the spell.
- These are always costly materials — the gold cost represents the ongoing price of casting.

**Examples of consumed components**:
- *Revivify*: 300 gp in diamonds (consumed).
- *Raise Dead*: 500 gp diamond (consumed).
- *Resurrection*: 1,000 gp diamond (consumed).
- *Greater Restoration*: 100 gp diamond dust (consumed).
- *Heroes' Feast*: Gem-encrusted bowl worth 1,000 gp (consumed).

### Practical Considerations

- **Buy an arcane focus or component pouch at character creation** — it covers 95% of material components.
- **Track costly components** on your character sheet. Ask your DM about availability.
- **Shield + Holy Symbol** works for Clerics and Paladins — the holy symbol can be emblazoned on the shield, leaving the other hand free.
- **Free hand rule**: You need at least one hand free for S and/or M components. If wielding a two-handed weapon, you can release one hand to cast (you only need two hands when attacking).
tags: spell components,verbal,somatic,material,arcane focus,holy symbol,component pouch,costly,consumed,free hand,war caster

title: Concentration
summary: How concentration works, what breaks it, the one-concentration-spell-at-a-time rule, and tips for maintaining concentration in combat.
content:
## Concentration

Many of the most powerful spells in D&D require **concentration** — ongoing mental focus to maintain the magical effect. If your concentration is broken, the spell ends immediately.

### How Concentration Works

- Some spells have a duration of "Concentration, up to X" (e.g., "Concentration, up to 1 minute").
- While concentrating, you maintain the spell's effect by devoting mental energy to it.
- You can **act normally** while concentrating — attack, move, cast cantrips, use items, etc.
- Concentration begins when you cast the spell and lasts up to the stated duration (or until broken).

### The One-Spell Rule

You can only concentrate on **one spell at a time**. If you cast a second concentration spell, the first one **immediately ends** — no save, no choice.

This is one of the most important rules in 5e. It prevents casters from stacking multiple powerful buffs or control effects simultaneously.

**Example**: You're concentrating on *Bless* (buffing your party). If you cast *Hold Person* (also concentration), *Bless* ends the moment you begin concentrating on *Hold Person*.

### What Breaks Concentration

#### 1. Taking Damage

When you take damage while concentrating on a spell, you must make a **Constitution saving throw** to maintain concentration.

- **DC = 10 or half the damage taken, whichever is higher.**
- If you take damage from multiple sources (e.g., *Magic Missile* with 3 darts), you make a **separate save for each source of damage**.
- Temporary hit points absorb damage before your real HP — if temp HP absorbs all the damage, you take 0 damage and don't need to make a concentration save.

**Examples**:
- Take 15 damage → DC is 10 (half of 15 is 7.5, rounded down to 7, but minimum is 10).
- Take 24 damage → DC is 12 (half of 24).
- Take 50 damage → DC is 25 (half of 50 — very hard to maintain).

#### 2. Being Incapacitated or Killed

- If you are **incapacitated** (stunned, paralyzed, unconscious, etc.), concentration is automatically broken.
- If you **die** or are **knocked unconscious** (drop to 0 HP), concentration ends immediately.

#### 3. Casting Another Concentration Spell

As mentioned above, casting a second concentration spell ends the first one.

#### 4. DM-Determined Environmental Effects

The DM may rule that extreme environmental effects (a tidal wave, being swallowed by a creature, an earthquake) require a concentration save or end concentration outright.

### What Does NOT Break Concentration

- Taking the Attack action or making weapon attacks.
- Moving, dashing, disengaging, or dodging.
- Casting a spell that does **not** require concentration.
- Casting a cantrip.
- Using class features or items.
- Being grappled or restrained (unless you're also incapacitated).
- Being frightened (you can still concentrate, though you have disadvantage on ability checks — but not saving throws).

### Tips for Maintaining Concentration

1. **Boost your Constitution saving throw**:
   - **War Caster feat**: Grants advantage on concentration saves. One of the best feats for any concentration caster.
   - **Resilient (Constitution)**: Grants proficiency in Constitution saves. Scales better at high levels than War Caster.
   - Both together make losing concentration very rare.

2. **Avoid taking damage**:
   - Stay at **range** — enemies can't hit you if they can't reach you.
   - Use **cover** (half cover = +2 AC, three-quarters cover = +5 AC).
   - Have allies **protect you** (Protection fighting style, Sentinel feat, grappling).

3. **Choose the right concentration spell**:
   - Don't concentrate on a low-impact spell when you might need a higher-impact one soon.
   - Spells that require an action each turn to maintain (like *Witch Bolt*) are generally worse than "cast and forget" concentration spells (like *Bless* or *Spirit Guardians*).

4. **Have a backup plan**:
   - Keep non-concentration spells ready for when your concentration spell is active.
   - Cantrips are always available and don't risk your concentration.

### Important Concentration Spells by Level

- **1st**: *Bless*, *Bane*, *Hex*, *Hunter's Mark*, *Entangle*, *Faerie Fire*
- **2nd**: *Hold Person*, *Moonbeam*, *Spiritual Weapon* (NOT concentration — a common misconception!), *Web*
- **3rd**: *Haste*, *Spirit Guardians*, *Hypnotic Pattern*, *Conjure Animals*
- **5th**: *Wall of Force*, *Conjure Elemental*, *Hold Monster*

title: Ritual Casting
summary: How ritual casting works: cast certain spells without using a spell slot by adding 10 minutes to the casting time. Which classes can ritual cast and what restrictions apply.
content:
## Ritual Casting

Ritual casting lets you cast certain spells without expending a spell slot — at the cost of extra time.

### How It Works

1. The spell must have the **ritual** tag (listed in its description).
2. Casting as a ritual adds **10 minutes** to the spell's normal casting time.
3. You **do not expend a spell slot** when casting a ritual.
4. You can still cast the spell normally (using a slot) if you need it faster.

### Who Can Ritual Cast?

Not every class can ritual cast. Each class that can has different rules:

| Class | Can Ritual Cast? | Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| Bard | Yes | Spell must be **known** by the bard |
| Cleric | Yes | Spell must be **prepared** |
| Druid | Yes | Spell must be **prepared** |
| Wizard | Yes | Spell must be in **spellbook** (does NOT need to be prepared) |
| Paladin | No | — |
| Ranger | No | — |
| Sorcerer | No | — |
| Warlock | Only with **Pact of the Tome** (Book of Ancient Secrets invocation) | Any ritual spell from any class list can be added to the Book of Shadows |

### Wizard: The Best Ritual Caster

The Wizard is uniquely powerful at ritual casting because:

- They do **not** need to have the spell prepared — it just needs to be in their spellbook.
- This means a Wizard can keep dozens of ritual spells in their spellbook and cast any of them as rituals without using prepared spell slots.
- Wizards should **always** copy any ritual spell they find into their spellbook, even if they wouldn't normally prepare it.

### Common Ritual Spells

| Spell | Level | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Detect Magic | 1st | Sense magic within 30 feet for 10 minutes |
| Identify | 1st | Learn the properties of a magic item |
| Find Familiar | 1st | Summon a loyal familiar (owl, cat, etc.) |
| Comprehend Languages | 1st | Understand any spoken/written language |
| Speak with Animals | 1st | Talk to beasts for 10 minutes |
| Gentle Repose | 2nd | Preserve a corpse, extend resurrection timer |
| Silence | 2nd | Create a 20-foot sphere of silence |
| Water Breathing | 3rd | Up to 10 creatures can breathe underwater for 24 hours |
| Leomund's Tiny Hut | 3rd | Create a dome shelter for rest |
| Commune | 5th | Ask your deity 3 yes/no questions |
| Contact Other Plane | 5th | Ask an entity 5 questions (risk of madness) |
| Rary's Telepathic Bond | 5th | Telepathic link between up to 8 creatures for 1 hour |

### When to Ritual Cast

- **Always** ritual cast when you have time and don't need the spell immediately.
- *Detect Magic* should almost always be ritual cast when exploring — save your slots.
- *Identify* after combat to learn about magic items — no rush, no slot needed.
- *Find Familiar* has a 1-hour casting time already, so the extra 10 minutes barely matters.
- *Water Breathing* and *Tiny Hut* are perfect ritual spells — you typically cast them before needing them.

### When NOT to Ritual Cast

- **In combat** or urgent situations — you can't afford an extra 10 minutes.
- When someone is **dying** and you need *Gentle Repose* RIGHT NOW (cast it normally).
- When **stealth** matters and the 10 minutes of chanting would alert enemies.

### Ritual Casting and Book of Ancient Secrets (Warlock)

Warlocks who take the **Pact of the Tome** and the **Book of Ancient Secrets** invocation gain:
- Two 1st-level ritual spells from **any class list** written into their Book of Shadows.
- The ability to copy additional ritual spells found during adventuring (from **any class list**, not just Warlock).
- These ritual spells don't count against spells known.
- This makes Warlocks potentially as versatile as Wizards for ritual casting, with even broader spell list access.
tags: ritual casting,ritual,spell slot,free cast,10 minutes,wizard,cleric,druid,bard,warlock,book of ancient secrets
